2004.assessment: Sudan was significantly isolated diplomatically due to terrorism designation (1993), US comprehensive sanctions, and Darfur crisis drawing international condemnation. However, Sudan maintained membership in the African Union, Arab League, UN, and IGAD. Relations with China, Gulf states, and Arab nations were normal. Regional isolation was partial.

sources.citation: ICG Reports on Sudan diplomatic relations, 2004

2005.assessment: CPA signing in January 2005 improved diplomatic standing somewhat. Sudan engaged with African Union on Darfur mission (AMIS deployed). UN Security Council attention to Sudan increased with UNSCR 1591. Relations with US remained hostile due to ICC process and Darfur. African and Arab diplomatic relations maintained.

sources.citation: UN Security Council, Sudan resolutions 2005

2006.assessment: Sudan's diplomatic isolation increased as Darfur crisis intensified and DPA failed. US expanded sanctions. Sudan rejected AU peacekeeping expansion. Bashir traveled regionally but faced ICC pressure. China maintained diplomatic shield in UN Security Council.

sources.citation: ICG Reports on Sudan, 2006

2007.assessment: Sudan accepted UNAMID deployment in July 2007 after AU pressure. This was a diplomatic concession. However, Bashir faced ICC process and Western nations increasingly treated Sudan as pariah. African and Arab diplomatic relations remained functional. China continued as key diplomatic patron.

sources.citation: UN Security Council Resolution 1769, UNAMID authorization, July 2007

2008.assessment: Diplomatic isolation deepened with ICC prosecutor indictment of Bashir (July 2008). Western nations became increasingly hostile. However, African Union called for suspension of ICC proceedings showing support in Global South. China, Russia, and Gulf states maintained normal relations. Sudan's isolation was primarily Western.

sources.citation: ICC Prosecutor vs. Bashir, July 2008

2009.assessment: ICC arrest warrant (March 2009) made Bashir formally wanted in ICC states. Sudan's diplomatic isolation from Western states was nearly complete. NGO expulsion (April 2009) drew broad international condemnation. AU, Arab League, and China continued to defend Sudan and oppose ICC proceedings. Bashir traveled to non-ICC-member states.

sources.citation: ICC Pre-Trial Chamber, Bashir arrest warrant, March 2009

2010.assessment: Sudan held internationally monitored elections. Carter Center and AU observed. Diplomatic engagement slightly increased around referendum preparations. Bashir remained wanted by ICC but traveled within Africa and Arab world. Isolation remained severe from Western perspective.

sources.citation: Carter Center, Sudan Elections 2010; AU Observer Mission

2011.assessment: South Sudan independence opened new diplomatic chapter. Sudan-South Sudan relations became bilateral with disputes over borders, oil, Abyei. AU engagement intensified for post-referendum management. New conflicts in South Kordofan and Blue Nile damaged diplomatic standing again. Bashir continued facing ICC warrants.

sources.citation: AU High-Level Implementation Panel, Sudan-South Sudan, 2011

2012.assessment: Sudan-South Sudan Heglig conflict in April 2012 drew international censure. AU pressured both sides. Sudan's isolation continued from Western states. Despite isolation, Sudan maintained normal relations with Arab League, China, Russia, and non-aligned states. UN Security Council engagement on multiple Sudan crises.

sources.citation: UN Security Council, Sudan statements 2012; AU Peace and Security Council

2013.assessment: September 2013 crackdown on protesters drew international condemnation. Western nations called for accountability. African and Arab relations maintained. Sudan under Bashir moved increasingly toward Gulf states for economic and diplomatic support. Russia and China continued to block UN Security Council measures.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, Sudan September 2013 protests report

2014.assessment: Sudan maintained regional African and Arab engagement. National Dialogue process attracted some AU support. Western nations maintained sanctions and diplomatic distance. Sudan continued participating in IGAD processes on South Sudan crisis (ironic given own crises). Bashir traveled to AU summits without ICC arrest.

sources.citation: African Union Peace and Security Council, Sudan reports 2014

2015.assessment: US began active engagement for sanctions relief, somewhat improving diplomatic standing. Sudan cooperated on counter-terrorism and was seen as potentially useful in migration crisis management. However, relations with EU remained strained due to Darfur and human rights. Bashir continued ICC evasion through non-member states.

sources.citation: US Department of State, Sudan Five-Track Engagement, 2014-2015

2016.assessment: US-Sudan engagement continued with progress on counter-terrorism cooperation. Bashir controversially visited China without ICC arrest. Sudan contributed troops to Saudi-led Yemen coalition, improving Gulf relations. Diplomatic standing improved slightly from absolute isolation but remained significantly isolated from Western states.

sources.citation: US State Department, Sudan Five-Track Engagement 2016

2017.assessment: Significant diplomatic improvement with partial US sanctions lift in October 2017. US-Sudan relations normalized significantly for trade and finance. However, terrorism list designation maintained. Bashir continued regional travel. Sudan-Turkey relations improved with Erdogan-Bashir partnership and Turkish military base on Suakin island.

sources.citation: US State Department, Sudan Engagement 2017; Reuters on Sudan-Turkey base deal

2018.assessment: Diplomatic standing stable from 2017 improvement but limited. US sanctions partly lifted but terrorism list maintained. Sudan engaged with Qatar (despite Saudi blockade) and UAE simultaneously, navigating Gulf tension. Domestic unrest starting in December 2018 attracted international attention with concerns about transition.

sources.citation: Reuters News, Sudan diplomacy 2018

2019.assessment: Diplomatic sea change after Bashir's ouster. Transitional government received broad international support. US began process toward terrorism list removal. 2020.assessment: Sudan fully reintegrated into international community in 2020. Terrorism list removal October 2020. Abraham Accords normalization with Israel. IMF/World Bank engagement. 2021.assessment: Military coup October 2021 sharply reversed diplomatic gains. US suspended $700M in financial assistance. African Union suspended Sudan's membership. EU suspended budget support. sources.citation: African Union Peace and Security Council, Sudan suspension, October 2021

2004.assessment: In 2004, the African Union Mission in Sudan (AMIS) began deployment in Darfur with approximately 300 military observers and a small protection force. This was invited and accepted by the Sudanese government. AMIS was limited in mandate and capacity. No other foreign military forces were present. Sudan maintained full sovereignty.

sources.citation: African Union Mission in Sudan (AMIS), established 2004

2005.assessment: AMIS expanded in Darfur to approximately 3,320 troops and 815 police by October 2005. The force was invited and operated under constraints. No UN peacekeepers in Sudan-proper (UNMIS established for south under CPA). Sudan maintained operational sovereignty.

sources.citation: African Union Mission in Sudan (AMIS) deployment data, 2005

2006.assessment: AMIS continued in Darfur. Sudan rejected proposals to transfer AMIS to UN control (UN "rehatting"). UNMIS in south under CPA. Sudan used its sovereignty argument to resist enhanced peacekeeping. Foreign military presence was limited and invited.

sources.citation: African Union Mission in Sudan (AMIS), 2006

2007.assessment: UNAMID (AU-UN hybrid mission) authorized by UNSCR 1769 in July 2007. Sudan agreed to accept UNAMID replacing AMIS. UNMIS continued in south. UNAMID deployment was slow due to government obstruction. By year-end, UNAMID was partially deployed in Darfur.

sources.citation: UN Security Council Resolution 1769, July 2007 — UNAMID authorization

2008.assessment: UNAMID fully authorized at up to 26,000 troops/police, but actual deployment was limited due to Sudanese obstruction. UNMIS continued in south. Sudan maintained formal sovereignty while the government obstructed effective peacekeeping operations.

sources.citation: UNAMID Deployment Statistics, 2008; UNMIS mandate

2009.assessment: UNAMID in Darfur at approximately 20,000 personnel (below authorized strength). Government NGO expulsion in April 2009 also reflected attitude toward international presence. UNMIS in south. Sudan tolerated but heavily constrained peacekeeping operations.

sources.citation: UNAMID Deployment Statistics, 2009

2010.assessment: UNAMID continued in Darfur. UNMIS preparing for post-referendum period in south. No other significant foreign military presence. Sudan maintained formal sovereignty over all territory with peacekeepers as tolerated but constrained guests.

sources.citation: UNAMID and UNMIS deployment data, 2010

2011.assessment: UNMIS terminated and replaced by UNMISS (for South Sudan) after independence in July 2011. UNISFA (UN Interim Security Force for Abyei) deployed for disputed Abyei area. UNAMID continued in Darfur. Multiple UN missions present but all with Sudan's formal consent.

sources.citation: UNSCR 1990 establishing UNISFA; UNMISS established for South Sudan

2012.assessment: UNAMID in Darfur, UNISFA in Abyei. These were invited missions constrained by government. Chinese engineers deployed within UNAMID framework. No uninvited foreign military presence. Sudan maintained sovereignty over all territory.

sources.citation: UNAMID and UNISFA deployment data, 2012

2013.assessment: UNAMID and UNISFA continued. Government began pressuring UNAMID to reduce presence. RSF increasingly deployed by government, partially filling security vacuum. No uninvited foreign military forces. Sudan maintained formal sovereignty.

sources.citation: UNAMID deployment data, 2013

2014.assessment: UNAMID continued in Darfur with reduced effectiveness and government pressure. Government refused helicopter access and movement in Darfur. Chinese state company investment brought some quasi-military security personnel. Sudan maintained formal sovereignty.

sources.citation: UNAMID deployment data, 2014; UN Secretary-General Reports on Sudan

2015.assessment: UNAMID continued. Sudan agreed to provide troops to Saudi-led Yemen coalition, meaning Sudanese forces deployed abroad. Russia expressed interest in military base in Sudan. UNAMID drawdown discussions began with Sudanese government. No uninvited foreign forces.

sources.citation: Reuters, Sudan troops in Yemen coalition, 2015

2016.assessment: UNAMID continued in Darfur though government pressed for drawdown. Sudan deployed troops in Yemen coalition. Russian naval base negotiations ongoing. Turkey secured Suakin island rehabilitation agreement. Multiple external military partnerships developing.

sources.citation: UNAMID deployment data, 2016; Reuters on Sudan-Turkey-Russia military relations

2017.assessment: UNAMID drawdown began. Russia-Sudan agreement signed for Russian naval base on Red Sea (Port Sudan). Turkey began rehabilitating Suakin island. Sudan continued Yemen coalition deployment. Multiple foreign military partnerships, all invited.

sources.citation: Reuters, Russia Sudan naval base agreement, November 2017

2018.assessment: UNAMID drawdown continued with reduced forces in Darfur. Russian base agreement continued negotiation. Sudan continued Yemen coalition. UAE established military infrastructure in Sudan. All foreign military presence was invited by Sudanese government.

sources.citation: UNAMID deployment data, 2018; Reuters on Sudan foreign military partnerships

2019.assessment: UNAMID further reduced. UAE and Saudi military advisors supported RSF during transition. Russia-Sudan base discussions continued under transitional government. Transitional government inherited multiple foreign military partnerships. No uninvited forces. UAE military presence in Sudan became a political factor during transition.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Sudan's Transition at Risk, December 2019

2020.assessment: UNAMID mandate ended December 2020. UN Security Council established UNITAMS (UN Integrated Transition Assistance Mission) for political support (no troops). Russia-Sudan naval base agreement ratified by Russian Duma. UAE military presence continued. Transitional government maintained multiple external security partnerships.

sources.citation: UNSCR 2524 establishing UNITAMS, June 2020; UNAMID mandate ended December 2020

2021.assessment: After October 2021 coup, UNITAMS continued political support role. UAE military presence maintained. Russia naval base framework in place. Hemeti's RSF connections with UAE deepened. No traditional foreign troops in combat roles, but external military support networks significant.

sources.citation: UNITAMS mandate; Reuters on UAE-RSF relations 2021

2022.assessment: Pre-war foreign military presence included UAE support for RSF (arms, training), Russian naval base framework in Sudan, and UNITAMS political mission. 2023.assessment: War created contested foreign military influence. UAE accused of supplying RSF through Libya air corridor. Russia supported SAF with military equipment. 2024.assessment: External military support to both SAF and RSF continued in 2024. UAE channeled weapons to RSF via Libya and Central African Republic. Russia supplied SAF. sources.citation: UN Panel of Experts on Sudan, 2024; Reuters Sudan reporting 2024

2025.assessment: External military support to both sides continued into 2025. No direct foreign troops in combat roles but weapons, advisors, and logistics support from UAE (RSF) and Russia/Egypt (SAF) were central to both sides' war-fighting capacity. The war's internationalization deepened.

2004.assessment: The Darfur crisis drove massive displacement in 2004. Over 1 million people displaced within Sudan (IDPs) from Darfur; hundreds of thousands fled to Chad. Sudan was simultaneously a major refugee-producing country (Darfur) and a refugee-hosting country (receiving from Ethiopia, Eritrea, South Sudan). Mass displacement year.

2005.assessment: Displacement continued at high levels from Darfur. CPA signature improved south-north displacement somewhat as SPLM-controlled areas stabilized. sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2005, Sudan; Internal Displacement Monitoring Centre

2006.assessment: High displacement from Darfur continued. DPA failure in May 2006 prevented refugee return. Chad-Sudan border region saw cross-border displacement. IDP numbers in Sudan remained among the world's highest at approximately 5-6 million.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2006, Sudan

2007.assessment: Mass displacement continued. UNAMID authorized but slow to deploy. New displacement events in Darfur added to already large IDP caseload. Some Sudanese refugees from conflict areas in Chad. Total displacement remained at extreme levels.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2007, Sudan

2008.assessment: Displacement continued at high but somewhat reduced levels from 2004 peak. UNAMID deployment provided some protection but could not halt all displacement. ICC indictment and related violence in Darfur caused additional displacement episodes. IDP stock remained very high.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2008, Sudan

2009.assessment: Displacement slightly reduced but still significant. NGO expulsion in April 2009 removed humanitarian organizations providing protection, likely increasing displacement vulnerability. ICC warrant year. IDP stock remained approximately 4.9 million Sudanese.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2009, Sudan

2010.assessment: New displacement reduced somewhat as Darfur conflict intensity moderated. Pre-referendum tensions and some return movements occurring. IDP stock remained very high. Sudan also hosting 350,000+ refugees from other countries (Ethiopia, Eritrea, Central African Republic).

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2010, Sudan

2011.assessment: South Sudan independence July 2011 triggered significant population movement — Sudanese with South Sudanese origins returned south; South Sudanese in north moved south. New conflicts in South Kordofan (June) and Blue Nile (September) added new IDP flows. Displacement remained at mass scale.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2011, Sudan

2012.assessment: New displacement from South Kordofan and Blue Nile conflicts. Sudan-South Sudan border fighting caused additional flows. Darfur displacement continued. Sudan had one of the largest IDP populations globally. Flight to Ethiopia and Chad continued.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2012, Sudan

2013.assessment: Displacement continued from multiple conflict theaters. September 2013 protests in Khartoum did not directly cause displacement but the economic crisis was pushing migrants abroad. IDP population remained approximately 2.5-3 million internally displaced from various conflicts.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2013, Sudan

2014.assessment: South Sudan civil war (erupted December 2013) sent 300,000+ South Sudanese refugees into Sudan, adding to Sudan's refugee hosting burden. Sudan's own conflicts continued generating displacement. Sudan became simultaneously a major origin and destination country for displacement.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2014, Sudan

2015.assessment: Displacement continued from Sudanese conflicts. Economic migration from Sudan to Libya (often en route to Europe) increased significantly as economic crisis deepened. Sudan's own conflicts generating displacement while also hosting refugees. Overall displacement significant.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2015, Sudan

2016.assessment: Sudan hosted approximately 900,000 registered refugees from South Sudan, Eritrea, Ethiopia, Central African Republic, and other countries. Sudanese displacement from ongoing conflicts continued. Net displacement situation complex with Sudan as both origin and major host.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2016, Sudan

2017.assessment: Sudan's displacement picture: approximately 1.8 million IDPs from Darfur and other conflicts; Sudan hosting approximately 1 million refugees. Displacement from violence in Jebel Marra (Darfur) added new flows. US partial sanctions lift had no impact on displacement.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2017, Sudan

2018.assessment: Sudan had approximately 2 million IDPs and was hosting approximately 1.1 million refugees. New displacement from Darfur inter-communal violence continued. Economic crisis driving labor migration. December 2018 protests did not immediately cause displacement but economic conditions pushing people to move.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2018, Sudan

2019.assessment: Transition year with some IDP return movements as political situation improved. Total IDP population gradually reducing in some areas. Sudan hosting approximately 1.1 million refugees. The transition created cautious hope for some displaced Sudanese to eventually return home.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2019, Sudan

2020.assessment: Flooding in 2020 caused significant additional displacement. Transitional government maintained refugee-hosting. JPA (October 2020) aimed to enable IDP return in Darfur. COVID-19 disrupted humanitarian operations. Sudan had approximately 1.5 million IDPs and hosted 1+ million refugees.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2020, Sudan

2021.assessment: El Geneina (West Darfur) ethnic violence (January 2021) caused mass displacement and refugee flows to Chad. Military coup October 2021 disrupted humanitarian programs. Sudan had approximately 1.8 million IDPs. New displacement from Darfur inter-communal violence significant.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2021, Sudan; IDMC Sudan IDP data 2021

2022.assessment: El Geneina ethnic violence continued generating new displacement in 2022. Sudan had approximately 1.9 million IDPs and was hosting over 1.1 million refugees. Anti-coup protest crackdown caused some urban displacement in Khartoum. Pre-war conditions with significant but not catastrophic displacement.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2022, Sudan

2023.assessment: Catastrophic displacement from April 2023 war. By December 2023, Sudan had over 6 million newly displaced people including 1.5 million cross-border refugees (to Chad, Egypt, CAR, Ethiopia, South Sudan) and 4.5+ million newly displaced internally. Sudan became the world's largest displacement crisis by mid-2024.

sources.citation: UNHCR Sudan Emergency, 2023; UN OCHA Sudan Crisis Reports, December 2023

2024.assessment: Displacement continued to grow in 2024. Total displaced Sudanese exceeded 11 million by late 2024 — the largest displacement crisis globally. Over 2 million cross-border refugees and 9+ million IDPs. Famine conditions in Darfur and North Sudan added to displacement pressures.

sources.citation: UNHCR Sudan Emergency Updates, 2024

2025.assessment: Displacement continued at catastrophic scale into 2025. War ongoing, famine spreading. Total displaced Sudanese remained well above 10 million with continued new displacement as fighting continued in Khartoum and Darfur areas.

sources.citation: UNHCR Sudan Emergency, early 2025

2004.assessment: Sudan faced comprehensive US sanctions since 1997 (Executive Order 13067) covering broad economic and financial transactions. The US designated Sudan a State Sponsor of Terrorism in 1993. UN Security Council Resolution 1591 (2005, upcoming) would add Darfur-specific sanctions. In 2004, US sanctions were comprehensive covering most trade and financial transactions between Sudan and US entities.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC, Sudan Sanctions Program

2005.assessment: UN Security Council imposed Darfur-specific sanctions in Resolution 1591 (March 2005), including travel bans and asset freezes for individuals responsible for atrocities. US comprehensive sanctions continued. Darfur-specific UN panel established. EU imposed own measures. Multi-layered sanctions regime in place.

sources.citation: UN Security Council Resolution 1591, March 2005
US Treasury OFAC Sudan Sanctions Program

2006.assessment: Darfur Peace Agreement failure in May 2006 led to calls for further sanctions. US expanded Sudan sanctions via the Darfur Peace and Accountability Act (signed October 2006). UN Darfur sanctions (UNSCR 1591) maintained. US comprehensive sanctions continued. Sudan remained heavily sanctioned.

sources.citation: US Darfur Peace and Accountability Act, October 2006

2007.assessment: US comprehensive sanctions maintained. UN Darfur sanctions continued. Executive Order 13400 (2006) and 13412 (2006) further expanded Sudan sanctions. Sudan was one of the most heavily sanctioned countries by the US. International financial institutions could not engage normally.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC Sudan Sanctions Program; UN Security Council Sudan sanctions

2008.assessment: Comprehensive US sanctions continued. UN Darfur sanctions maintained with periodic reviews. ICC arrest warrant process (indictment July 2008 for Bashir) increased international pressure but did not immediately add new formal sanctions. Sudan remained isolated from US/EU financial systems.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC Sudan Sanctions; ICC Prosecutor vs. Bashir, July 2008

2009.assessment: ICC arrest warrant for Bashir (March 2009) increased international pressure. US comprehensive sanctions continued. After Bashir expelled major NGOs, international pressure for additional measures mounted but formal sanctions additions were limited. UNSCR 1591 Darfur sanctions maintained.

sources.citation: ICC Pre-Trial Chamber, Decision on the Prosecution's Application for Arrest Warrant for Bashir, March 2009

2010.assessment: Comprehensive US sanctions continued despite Sudan elections and CPA implementation. Sudan remained on US State Sponsors of Terrorism list. UN Darfur sanctions maintained. Pre-referendum period saw some diplomatic activity but no sanctions relief.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC Sudan Sanctions Program

2011.assessment: US comprehensive sanctions continued. South Sudan's independence slightly complicated the picture — sanctions were adjusted to apply to Sudan (north) only. New ICC warrant for Bashir (crimes against humanity, July 2010 and genocide, July 2011) added to international pressure. UN Darfur sanctions maintained.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC Sudan Sanctions Program; ICC second Bashir warrant, July 2010

2012.assessment: US comprehensive sanctions maintained. Sudan-South Sudan conflicts in 2012 increased international pressure. UN Darfur sanctions continued. Sudan remained on terrorism list. No sanctions relief despite CPA implementation and South Sudan independence.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC Sudan Sanctions Program

2013.assessment: US comprehensive sanctions continued with no relief. September 2013 protest crackdown with 170+ killed drew sharp international criticism and calls for more sanctions. UN Darfur sanctions maintained. Sudan isolated from Western financial systems.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC Sudan Sanctions Program

2014.assessment: Comprehensive US sanctions continued. UN Darfur sanctions maintained. Sudan remained on terrorism list preventing debt relief through IMF/World Bank HIPC. No pathway to sanctions relief under Bashir's NCP with ICC warrant outstanding.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC Sudan Sanctions Program

2015.assessment: US began tentative engagement with Sudan on sanctions relief in exchange for cooperation on counter-terrorism and migration. Obama administration initiated a policy review. However, no formal sanctions changes occurred in 2015. Comprehensive US sanctions continued.

sources.citation: US Department of State, Sudan Policy Review, 2014-2015

2016.assessment: US-Sudan engagement continued with five tracks of cooperation. Sudan cooperated on counter-terrorism, anti-trafficking, and reduced Darfur military operations. US indicated willingness to consider sanctions relief if progress maintained. Comprehensive sanctions still in place throughout 2016.

sources.citation: US Department of State, Sudan Five-Track Engagement

2017.assessment: Major change: US partially lifted comprehensive Sudan sanctions in October 2017 (Executive Order 13761 revocation and General License) covering most trade and financial transactions. However, Sudan remained on the State Sponsors of Terrorism list, preventing full normalization. UN Darfur-specific sanctions maintained. A significant but partial sanctions relief.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC, General License No. 1 under the Sudan Sanctions Regulations, October 2017

2018.assessment: Post-partial-lift sanctions environment. Most US-Sudan economic transactions now legal, but terrorism list designation prevented full normalization. Banks still cautious about Sudan transactions due to remaining legal uncertainty. UN Darfur individual sanctions maintained. Limited improvement in practice despite formal sanctions partial lift.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC Sudan Sanctions; State Department Sudan terrorism designation

2019.assessment: Transition year. After Bashir's ouster, transitional government began engagement with US on terrorism list removal — a prerequisite for debt relief and full financial normalization. Most US trade sanctions remained lifted (from 2017). Individual sanctions on NCP/Bashir figures added. UN Darfur sanctions maintained.

sources.citation: US State Department Sudan engagement, 2019

2020.assessment: Major milestone: Sudan removed from US State Sponsors of Terrorism list in October 2020 after agreeing to normalize relations with Israel and paying compensation to US Embassy bombing victims. This opened the path to HIPC debt relief. US general trade sanctions remained lifted. UN Darfur sanctions continued but diminished in practical impact.

sources.citation: US State Department, Sudan Removal from State Sponsors of Terrorism List, October 2020

2021.assessment: Military coup in October 2021 complicated sanctions picture. US and international community threatened to re-impose sanctions. IMF/World Bank suspended debt relief processes. However, no formal re-imposition of comprehensive sanctions occurred — rather, financial assistance was suspended. UN Darfur sanctions maintained.

sources.citation: US Department of State, Statement on Sudan Coup, October 2021

2022.assessment: Sanctions status stable after coup. No re-imposition of comprehensive US sanctions but no new financial support either. UN Darfur targeted sanctions maintained. Sudan essentially in a sanctions gray zone — formal measures limited but financial isolation continued through suspended international programs.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC Sudan Sanctions Program; UN Security Council Sudan sanctions

2023.assessment: SAF-RSF war triggered new sanctions measures. US imposed targeted sanctions on SAF and RSF military leaders and companies under Executive Order 13067 (Sudan) and new Darfur-specific authorities. EU added additional individuals. Mass atrocities in Darfur led to calls for arms embargo expansion. Targeted individual/entity sanctions regime expanded significantly.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC, Sudan-Related Designations, 2023

2024.assessment: Sanctions expanded further in 2024 targeting SAF and RSF companies, individuals, and gold trading networks funding the war. US, EU, UK all added measures. The RSF's gold smuggling through UAE was targeted. However, no comprehensive economic embargo was reimposed — sanctions remained targeted at conflict actors.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC, Sudan Designations, 2024; EU Sudan sanctions, 2024

2025.assessment: Targeted sanctions on war-related actors continued into 2025. No comprehensive economic embargo imposed. Both SAF and RSF entities under various US/EU targeted measures. War economy continued to function through Gulf state connections not covered by targeted measures.

sources.citation: Reuters News, Sudan sanctions reporting 2025

2004.assessment: Sudan received the worst possible Freedom House Civil Liberties score of 7 in 2004. Press was tightly controlled, political opposition suppressed, assembly restricted, and the Darfur conflict saw mass human rights violations against civilians. The NCP security state operated with total impunity.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2005, Sudan

2005.assessment: CPA created new Interim National Constitution with nominal civil liberties guarantees, but implementation remained limited. The security state continued to suppress dissent. Darfur remained a zone of mass atrocity. Press freedom minimal; assembly and movement highly restricted, especially in conflict zones. FH CL remained at 7.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2006, Sudan

2006.assessment: Civil liberties severely restricted throughout Sudan. Darfur residents faced systematic violence and displacement. Press censorship maintained by security authorities. Political opposition activists subjected to arbitrary detention. Religious freedom limited for non-Muslims. Freedom House rated Sudan Not Free with CL=7.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2007, Sudan

2007.assessment: Civil liberties at worst possible level. Arbitrary detention by NISS, forced disappearances, and torture documented. Press faced prior censorship. Civil society heavily restricted. The conflict in Darfur continued to cause mass civilian suffering. Freedom House CL=7 for Sudan.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2008, Sudan

2008.assessment: Civil liberties remained at worst possible level. NISS had sweeping powers to detain without charge. Press censorship continued. Public assembly banned. The JEM attack on Omdurman led to mass arrests of Darfuris in Khartoum. ICC indictment process demonstrated scale of rights violations.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2009, Sudan

2009.assessment: ICC arrest warrant for Bashir (March 2009) for war crimes and crimes against humanity documented scale of state-sponsored violence. NGO expulsion in April 2009 increased humanitarian suffering. Civil liberties continued at worst level with systematic repression, press censorship, and restriction of assembly. FH CL=7.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2010, Sudan

2010.assessment: Elections held April 2010 were deeply flawed with press restrictions and political opposition harassment. Civil liberties remained at worst level. Despite CPA provisions, fundamental freedoms not protected in practice. FH CL=7, Not Free. Security forces continued arbitrary detentions.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2011, Sudan

2011.assessment: Civil liberties at worst level following South Sudan's independence. NCP consolidated control. New conflicts in South Kordofan and Blue Nile brought new atrocities. Protests in Khartoum inspired by Arab Spring quickly suppressed. FH CL=7. Religious minorities faced increasing pressure.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2012, Sudan

2012.assessment: Civil liberties continued at worst possible level. Student protests suppressed. Media under severe censorship with pre-publication review. NGOs faced harassment. Women's rights activists prosecuted under public order laws. No freedom of peaceful assembly. FH CL=7.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2013, Sudan

2013.assessment: September 2013 protests over fuel subsidy removal were violently suppressed with 170+ deaths. This was a major civil liberties crisis. NISS detained journalists and activists. No freedom of assembly in practice. FH CL=7.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2014, Sudan
Human Rights Watch, Sudan: Mass Killings of Protesters, September 2013

2014.assessment: Civil liberties remained at worst level. Journalists and activists regularly detained. Women's rights activists prosecuted. No freedom of peaceful assembly. The National Security Act gave NISS sweeping detention powers without judicial review. FH CL=7.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2015, Sudan

2015.assessment: Elections boycotted by major opposition; environment of fear and harassment. Civil society and media under severe restrictions. Chemical weapons alleged in Darfur. Freedom House CL=7. Political prisoners including student leaders held. No progress on civil liberties.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2016, Sudan

2016.assessment: Civil liberties at worst possible level. Internet shutdowns to suppress protests. Journalists and activists regularly detained. Torture documented in NISS detention. Women's rights activists prosecuted. FH CL=7, Not Free.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2017, Sudan

2017.assessment: Civil liberties remained at worst level despite US partial sanctions lift. Press continued under censorship. NISS continued arbitrary arrests. The sanctions lift did not reflect improvements in civil liberties. FH CL=7.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2018, Sudan

2018.assessment: Protest movement that began December 2018 was met with violent repression. Security forces used live ammunition; dozens killed. Internet services repeatedly cut. Journalists and protest leaders detained. Civil liberties at worst possible level. FH CL=7.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2019, Sudan

2019.assessment: Transition year: Bashir ousted April 2019. Constitutional Document guaranteed civil liberties. Press freedom improved dramatically with independent media emerging. Political prisoners released. However, RSF massacre of protesters at Khartoum sit-in (June 3) killed 128+. Freedom House upgraded Sudan CL from 7 to 6, and status changed to Partly Free.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2020, Sudan

2020.assessment: Civil liberties improved significantly under PM Hamdok's transitional government. Press constraints eased. Political assembly generally permitted. Women's rights protections strengthened. However, RSF committed violations in conflict areas. FH CL improved to 5; Sudan Partly Free.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2021, Sudan

2021.assessment: Military coup October 2021 reversed civil liberties gains. Protesters shot by security forces; dozens killed in anti-coup demonstrations. Internet shutdowns deployed. Journalists detained. Emergency laws imposed. Freedom House downgraded Sudan CL from 5 to 6. Status returned to Not Free.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2022, Sudan

2022.assessment: Military junta continued repression of anti-coup protesters throughout 2022. Over 100 protesters killed by security forces. Press restrictions reimposed. Internet disruptions used to suppress coordination. Civil society and political activists systematically targeted. FH CL=6, Not Free.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2023, Sudan

2023.assessment: SAF-RSF war erupted April 2023, causing catastrophic civil liberties collapse. Mass atrocities including rape as weapon of war, mass killings, ethnic cleansing in Darfur by RSF. No civil society or press freedom. Freedom House returned Sudan to CL=7, Not Free.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2024, Sudan

2024.assessment: Civil liberties at worst possible level throughout 2024. Mass atrocities continued in Darfur. No functioning civil society. Journalists unable to operate. Famine conditions in conflict areas. Infrastructure destroyed. FH CL=7, Not Free.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2025, Sudan

2025.assessment: War continued into 2025 with no improvement in civil liberties. Both SAF and RSF committed violations in their areas of control. No free press, assembly, or civil society in war zones. SAF-controlled Port Sudan saw limited civil life but under military governance without genuine civil liberties.

sources.citation: Reuters News, Sudan 2025 reporting

2004.assessment: The NCP under Bashir maintained relatively unified control of the Sudanese state, with Bashir's inner circle managing the Darfur conflict through a combination of military force and Janjaweed militia alliances. The ruling coalition was cohesive around suppressing opposition, though tensions existed between military, security, and NCP party factions. No significant coup risk.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Sudan's Other Wars, June 2003

2005.assessment: CPA created formal power-sharing between NCP and SPLM but these were two distinct elite factions with competing agendas. Within the NCP itself, there were tensions between Bashir's military faction and Turabi's Islamist network (Turabi already expelled in 1999). The SPLM-NCP divide was managed within the GNU framework but tensions were constant.

sources.citation: ICG Report: The Khartoum-SPLM Agreement: Sudan's Uncertain Peace, July 2005

2006.assessment: NCP under Bashir maintained internal cohesion around resistance to ICC and continuation of Darfur counter-insurgency. The CPA framework held despite tensions with SPLM. No significant factional splits within NCP's inner circle. Turabi's Popular Congress Party remained marginalized.

sources.citation: ICG qualitative assessment based on Sudan political reports 2006

2007.assessment: NCP inner circle remained cohesive. Bashir retained firm control over party and military. The decision to accept UNAMID deployment (though delayed and obstructed) showed elite consensus on managing external pressure. NCP-SPLM tensions rose over CPA implementation but no breakdown.

sources.citation: ICG qualitative assessment based on Sudan political reports 2007

2008.assessment: NCP maintained internal cohesion in face of ICC arrest warrant process. Bashir rallied party behind him using the ICC as external threat. The Omdurman rebel attack created brief shock but consolidated NCP unity. JEM attack was used to justify continued security spending and elite cohesion around counter-insurgency.

sources.citation: ICG qualitative assessment based on Sudan political reports 2008-2009

2009.assessment: ICC arrest warrant consolidated NCP elite loyalty to Bashir through external threat narrative. The expulsion of NGOs demonstrated cohesive decision-making. NCP retained firm control with Bashir at center. No significant factional splits within ruling elite.

sources.citation: ICG qualitative assessment based on Sudan political reports 2009

2010.assessment: NCP won the April 2010 elections and Bashir was re-elected. SPLM of John Garang (later Salva Kiir) participated. NCP internal cohesion maintained. Preparations for South Sudan referendum created potential for post-independence NCP adjustment but leadership was united on managing the transition.

sources.citation: Carter Center, Sudan 2010 Elections Report

2011.assessment: South Sudan's independence forced NCP to manage a major transition. Loss of oil revenue threatened the economic basis of patronage politics. New conflicts (South Kordofan, Blue Nile) tested NCP military leadership. However, Bashir maintained firm grip on ruling coalition, which adapted to smaller post-CPA Sudan.

sources.citation: ICG qualitative assessment based on Sudan political reports 2011

2012.assessment: Economic pressures from oil revenue loss tested NCP elite cohesion. Tensions between military, security, and party factions emerged over resource distribution. NCP engaged in Sudan-South Sudan border conflict at Heglig. Bashir maintained control through patronage but economic strain created underlying tensions.

sources.citation: ICG qualitative assessment based on Sudan political reports 2012

2013.assessment: September 2013 urban protests and brutal suppression revealed cracks in NCP governance. First VP Ali Osman Taha (once considered heir apparent) was demoted in late 2013. Tensions between Bashir and other NCP factions intensified. The regime's response to economic crisis was incoherent, suggesting factional competition. A significant purge of NCP reformers occurred.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Sudan's Coup: Inevitable?, October 2019 (historical analysis)

2014.assessment: NCP remained in power but showed increasing internal tensions. Multiple armed conflicts stretched state capacity. Bashir increasingly relied on RSF (Rapid Support Forces under Hemeti) as alternative security force outside normal military command — this created a parallel power center. Economic crisis deepened.

sources.citation: ICG qualitative assessment based on Sudan political reports 2014

2015.assessment: Bashir re-elected with 94% in boycotted elections. RSF under Hemeti continued to grow as a parallel military force. NCP internal elections showed some competition but Bashir retained firm control. SAF and RSF were increasingly distinct power centers under formal NCP authority.

sources.citation: ICG qualitative assessment based on Sudan political reports 2015

2016.assessment: NCP ruling coalition showed significant internal tensions. RSF under Hemeti had expanded greatly from Darfur deployments into gold mining and other economic activities, creating an autonomous economic base. Bashir's management of multiple competing factions (military, NISS, RSF, party) required constant balancing. No open split but underlying fragility.

sources.citation: ICG qualitative assessment based on Sudan political reports 2016

2017.assessment: Increasing elite tensions within the ruling coalition. Bashir became more dependent on RSF under Hemeti, who was appointed Deputy Head of Transitional Sovereignty Council in later 2019 — indicating his rise. Economic crisis deepened, reducing the patronage base available to Bashir to manage competing elites. Military-NCP-security tensions grew.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Sudan's Transition at Risk, December 2019 (historical analysis)

2018.assessment: Protest movement beginning December 2018 revealed fractured elite response. Security services disagreed on how to handle protests. SAF under Burhan began distancing from Bashir as protests intensified. RSF under Hemeti positioned as alternative pillar. Bashir's NCP increasingly isolated as economic collapse undermined patronage.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Sudan's Coup: Inevitable?, October 2019

2019.assessment: Major elite fracture in 2019. SAF (Burhan) and RSF (Hemeti) united to oust Bashir on April 11, then turned against civilian protesters (June 3 massacre). TSC was a fragile elite coalition between SAF and RSF with very different long-term interests. The civilian-military partnership in the August 2019 Constitutional Document created a three-way elite structure (SAF, RSF, civilians) with unresolved tensions.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Sudan's Transition at Risk, December 2019

2020.assessment: Elite cohesion was fragile but functional under the civilian-military transitional arrangement. PM Hamdok led cabinet while TSC (Burhan and Hemeti) held formal sovereignty. Tensions between civilian and military components were managed through JPA and Framework processes. SAF-RSF tensions were temporarily suppressed but structural rivalry remained.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Safeguarding Sudan's Transition, October 2020

2021.assessment: Elite tensions escalated throughout 2021. In September 2021 a SAF-backed counter-coup attempt failed. Burhan launched the October 25 coup dissolving civilian government. This represented open conflict between military elite and civilian elite. The SAF-RSF partnership held for the coup but their alliance was increasingly tense over post-coup arrangements.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Sudan's Coup and the Perils Ahead, November 2021

2022.assessment: SAF (Burhan) and RSF (Hemeti) maintained uneasy alliance in 2022 while civilian forces organized. Fundamental disagreements over RSF integration into SAF (the key trigger for the 2023 war) were already visible. Framework Agreement with civilian FFC in December 2022 was opposed by Hemeti who wanted different terms. Elite division was structural and growing.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Sudan: Reversing the Dangerous Fragmentation of Power, December 2022

2023.assessment: Complete elite fragmentation. SAF-RSF war erupted April 15, 2023 after negotiations over RSF integration collapsed. Sudan's elite split into two armed camps: SAF (Burhan) controlling the formal state and north, and RSF (Hemeti) controlling Khartoum and Darfur. No unified ruling coalition; armed conflict replaced political competition.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Sudan's Spreading War, November 2023

2024.assessment: Complete elite fragmentation continued throughout 2024. SAF and RSF remained at war with no negotiations. Hemeti sought international recognition of RSF administration; Burhan maintained the SAF government in Port Sudan. International mediation (US, Saudi, IGAD) failed to achieve ceasefire. Two incompatible elite structures.

sources.citation: ACLED Sudan Crisis Summary 2024; Reuters Sudan reporting 2024

2025.assessment: Complete elite fragmentation continued into 2025. SAF launched counteroffensives and made some gains in Khartoum area. RSF retained Darfur and portions of greater Khartoum. No political settlement on the horizon. Both military factions showed internal cohesion within their own structures but no prospect of national elite unification.

sources.citation: Reuters News, Sudan war updates 2025

2004.assessment: Sudan under Bashir's NCP was an authoritarian state with nominal legislative and judicial institutions that served regime interests. The National Assembly had no meaningful independence. Courts were subordinate to the executive. The Darfur conflict demonstrated the regime's use of extrajudicial militias (Janjaweed) in total disregard of any rule of law framework.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2005, Sudan

2005.assessment: The Comprehensive Peace Agreement (CPA) created a new transitional constitution with power-sharing between NCP and SPLM. This nominally increased institutional diversity but the NCP retained dominant control of key institutions in the north. The Government of National Unity had SPLM participation but NCP ran core ministries. Courts and bureaucracy remained under NCP control.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2006, Sudan

2006.assessment: Government of National Unity continued with nominal institutional structures but NCP dominance. National legislature functioned as rubber stamp. CPA implementation was contested. In Darfur, governance was completely absent with military and militia forces substituting for civilian administration. International assessment consistently rated Sudan as Not Free.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2007, Sudan

2007.assessment: Institutional functioning remained severely degraded under Bashir-NCP rule. The Government of National Unity (GNU) existed on paper with SPLM ministers but genuine power-sharing was absent. The judiciary was not independent. Elections were planned for 2009 but repeatedly postponed. Administrative capacity outside Khartoum was weak.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2008, Sudan

2008.assessment: Continued authoritarian governance under NCP/Bashir with nominally functioning institutions. The parliament served regime interests. State security apparatus (NISS) operated outside judicial constraints. International monitors consistently rated Sudan's institutions as severely degraded. The Darfur crisis and Omdurman rebel attack tested state capacity.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2009, Sudan

2009.assessment: ICC arrest warrant for Bashir (March 2009) demonstrated international legal community's assessment that the regime was conducting war crimes with full institutional complicity. NCP governance structures functioned primarily as instruments of regime survival. Expulsion of NGOs showed institutional capacity to make major decisions but with no accountability.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2010, Sudan

2010.assessment: Sudan held multi-party elections in April 2010. International observers noted widespread fraud and intimidation. NCP won with 68% of the vote, Bashir re-elected as president. Elections demonstrated nominal institutional processes but without meaningful democratic functioning. Freedom House PR rating: 7, CL rating: 7 (worst possible).

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2011, Sudan
Carter Center, Sudan April 2010 Elections Report

2011.assessment: South Sudan independence reduced Sudan's territory and removed SPLM from the GNU. NCP consolidated authoritarian control in remaining Sudan. New conflicts in South Kordofan and Blue Nile showed continued disregard for rule of law in periphery. Khartoum-based institutions functioned but only in service of regime. No independent judiciary.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2012, Sudan

2012.assessment: Continued severe authoritarian governance. South Sudan independence reduced oil revenues (most oil fields were in the south), creating economic pressure. Government institutions functioned primarily as mechanisms for NCP political control and resource extraction. No independent legislature, judiciary, or accountability mechanisms.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2013, Sudan

2013.assessment: September 2013 urban protests brutally suppressed; dozens killed by security forces. NCP used state institutions for repression. No accountability mechanisms functioned. Parliament was loyal to Bashir. Courts did not challenge regime actions. Deepening economic crisis led to subsidy cuts that triggered protests.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2014, Sudan

2014.assessment: NCP-NCP-dominated governance continued. National Dialogue initiative announced by Bashir but most opposition rejected it as cosmetic. The National Security Law gave broad extrajudicial powers to NISS. Multiple political prisoners held without trial. Institutions functioned as instruments of NCP rule with no genuine checks.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2015, Sudan

2015.assessment: Sudan held elections in April 2015 boycotted by most opposition parties. Bashir won with 94% of the vote. NCP continued to dominate all branches of government. Institutions had some capacity (bureaucracy delivered services in Khartoum) but no independence from executive. Freedom House rated Sudan 7/7 (worst) on both Political Rights and Civil Liberties.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2016, Sudan

2016.assessment: National Dialogue process formally concluded in October 2016 but without meaningful participation from major armed movements or opposition. NCP continued as dominant political force. Institutions remained severely degraded with no meaningful checks on executive power. NISS operated as primary tool of political control.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2017, Sudan

2017.assessment: NCP-dominated authoritarian rule continued under Bashir. Institutions remained tools of political control. The partial US sanctions lift in October 2017 indicated some external recognition of limited improvements, but institutional quality was unchanged. No genuine separation of powers, independent judiciary, or free press existed.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2018, Sudan

2018.assessment: Protest movement began December 2018, challenging the regime. State institutions including security services were used to suppress protests. Courts provided no protection to detained protesters. Parliament did not represent opposition. The regime's response to protests demonstrated the purely instrumental nature of state institutions.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2019, Sudan

2019.assessment: Major institutional transition in 2019. Bashir ousted April 11; NCP dissolved; NISS restructured into General Intelligence Service (GIS). The Constitutional Document (August 2019) created a hybrid Transitional Sovereignty Council (TSC), a Cabinet of Ministers under a civilian PM, and a planned Legislative Council (never formed). Institutional functioning improved from Bashir era but remained fragile with SAF and RSF holding real power.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Sudan's Transition at Risk, December 2019
Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2020, Sudan

2020.assessment: Transitional civilian government under PM Hamdok made progress on institutional reform. Cabinet of technocratic ministers began governance improvements. Juba Peace Agreement expanded representation. However, the Transitional Legislative Council (required by Constitutional Document) was never formed, leaving no legislative branch. Security institutions (SAF, RSF) retained autonomous power outside civilian control.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2021, Sudan
ICG Report: Safeguarding Sudan's Transition, October 2020

2021.assessment: Military coup on October 25, 2021 by General al-Burhan dissolved the civilian transitional government. The Cabinet of Ministers was dissolved; PM Hamdok briefly arrested and later resigned in January 2022. The Transitional Sovereignty Council was reconstituted as military-only. All civilian institutional gains of the 2019-2021 period were reversed. Institutions collapsed to severely degraded status under military junta.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Sudan's Coup and the Perils Ahead, November 2021
Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2022, Sudan

2022.assessment: Military junta ruled Sudan. Framework Agreement negotiations between junta and civilian Forces for Freedom and Change (FFC) occurred throughout 2022 but yielded only a signed framework in December 2022, with final agreement pending. No legislative body functioned. Security institutions (SAF, RSF) operated outside judicial or civilian oversight.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2023, Sudan

2023.assessment: War between SAF and RSF erupted April 2023, effectively destroying what remained of state institutions in Khartoum. Courts, ministries, and bureaucratic offices became inaccessible or destroyed. The SAF government relocated to Port Sudan and maintained nominal functions. No legislative body existed. Two competing armed forces claimed authority with no functioning civilian institutions.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Sudan's Spreading War, November 2023

2024.assessment: SAF maintained a rump government in Port Sudan with some ministerial functions. RSF established parallel administration in areas it controlled. No unified state institutions existed. No legislative body, no functioning judiciary outside Port Sudan environs. The state had effectively fragmented with two competing authorities and no legitimate civilian governance.

sources.citation: ACLED Sudan Crisis Summary 2024; Reuters Sudan reporting 2024

2025.assessment: The SAF-RSF war continued into 2025 with no political settlement. The SAF government in Port Sudan maintained some administrative capacity in SAF-controlled territory. No national elections, no legislature, and no functioning national institutions. The state remained bifurcated and failing.

sources.citation: Reuters News, Sudan reporting 2025

2004.assessment: The Darfur conflict was at peak intensity in 2004. Government forces and Janjaweed militias conducted large-scale attacks on villages, causing mass civilian casualties. Estimates of deaths from conflict and atrocity-related causes in Darfur alone ran to tens of thousands. This was one of the most violent years in Sudan's recent history.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), Sudan, 2004
Human Rights Watch, Darfur Destroyed, May 2004

2005.assessment: CPA signed January 2005 ended the north-south war, but Darfur fighting continued. Violence was high though somewhat below the 2004 peak. Janjaweed attacks on villages and rebel fighting with government forces continued. South Sudan transition period was relatively calm under CPA framework.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Sudan, 2005

2006.assessment: Darfur conflict continued at high levels. The Darfur Peace Agreement of May 2006 was rejected by major rebel factions, and fighting intensified. Inter-rebel clashes also increased. Government forces and Janjaweed continued attacks. Violence in Darfur remained at high levels with thousands of conflict-related deaths.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Sudan, 2006

2007.assessment: Darfur conflict continued with significant violence. UNAMID authorized but slow to deploy. Inter-rebel conflicts added to complexity. Chad border area saw cross-border attacks. Violence remained at thousands of deaths per year in Darfur. Eastern Sudan was relatively stable following the Eastern Sudan Peace Agreement.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Sudan, 2007

2008.assessment: Darfur conflict continued at high levels. In May 2008, the rebel movement JEM launched a major attack reaching Omdurman, the first rebel strike near Khartoum. This demonstrated escalation beyond western Sudan. UCDP estimates Sudan conflict deaths in the thousands in 2008. Aerial bombardment by SAF in Darfur continued.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Sudan, 2008

2009.assessment: Darfur conflict continued with significant violence. Government expelled major NGOs after ICC arrest warrant for Bashir in March 2009, worsening humanitarian conditions. Violence continued with thousands of deaths, though reduced from peak 2004 levels. Sudan-Chad relations remained tense with cross-border attacks.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Sudan, 2009

2010.assessment: Darfur violence continued but at reduced levels. Some rebel factions engaged in peace talks. Sudan held elections in April 2010. Preparations for South Sudan's January 2011 independence referendum created some tension in border areas. Overall organized violence was lower than peak years but still significant.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Sudan, 2010

2011.assessment: South Sudan independence in July 2011 was relatively peaceful. However, new conflicts erupted: fighting in South Kordofan (June 2011) and Blue Nile (September 2011) between SPLM-N and SAF caused significant casualties. Darfur violence continued. Multiple simultaneous conflict fronts made this a high-violence year for Sudan.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Sudan, 2011

2012.assessment: Violence continued in South Kordofan, Blue Nile, and Darfur simultaneously. Sudan-South Sudan military clashes over Heglig oil field in April 2012. Government aerial bombardments in Nuba Mountains. Multiple conflict fronts kept violence at high levels throughout the year.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Sudan, 2012

2013.assessment: Conflicts in Darfur, South Kordofan, and Blue Nile continued. September 2013 saw major urban protests in Khartoum against fuel subsidy cuts; security forces killed dozens of protesters. ICG estimated hundreds of thousands affected by ongoing violence. Multiple fronts maintained high death tolls.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Sudan's Spreading Conflict, Part I, February 2013

2014.assessment: Armed conflicts continued in Darfur, South Kordofan, and Blue Nile. Government offensives in Nuba Mountains. Inter-communal violence in Darfur intensified. UCDP data shows thousands of conflict deaths across multiple theaters. Aerial bombardments of civilian areas documented by human rights monitors.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Sudan, 2014

2015.assessment: Conflicts in Darfur, South Kordofan, and Blue Nile continued. Government claimed some areas were secured. Violence in Darfur was episodic with significant mass displacement events. UCDP records thousands of conflict deaths across the country. Nuba Mountains fighting continued.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Sudan, 2015

2016.assessment: Violence in Darfur fluctuated; inter-communal conflict intensified particularly between Arab tribes. South Kordofan and Blue Nile fighting continued. Government declared unilateral ceasefires but violations documented. UCDP estimates place conflict deaths in the hundreds to low thousands.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Sudan, 2016

2017.assessment: Violence in Sudan reduced somewhat compared to earlier years. UCDP and ACLED data show continued fighting in Darfur, South Kordofan, and Blue Nile but at lower intensity. US partially lifted sanctions in October 2017 after assessing reduced conflict. Inter-communal violence in Darfur continued to cause civilian deaths.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Sudan, 2017; ACLED Data for Sudan, 2017

2018.assessment: Organized armed conflict in Darfur and other peripheries continued at reduced levels from earlier peaks. Protests against economic conditions and Bashir's rule began in December 2018, with security forces killing protesters. ACLED recorded dozens of fatalities from protest crackdowns in late 2018.

sources.citation: ACLED Data for Sudan, 2018

2019.assessment: Major political violence in 2019: RSF (under Hemeti) massacred protesters at the Khartoum sit-in on June 3, killing at least 128 people. Protests and crackdowns killed hundreds across the transition period. Peripheral conflicts (Darfur, South Kordofan) continued at lower levels. The regime change involved significant state-sponsored violence against civilians.

sources.citation: ACLED Data for Sudan, 2019; Amnesty International, Sudan: RSF Forces Kill Protesters, June 2019
Amnesty International, Sudan: RSF Forces Kill Dozens of Peaceful Protesters, June 2019

2020.assessment: Violence reduced significantly from 2019 levels as the transitional government under Prime Minister Hamdok pursued peace. Juba Peace Agreement signed October 2020 brought most armed movements on board. Inter-communal violence in Darfur and Jebel Marra area continued. Overall organized conflict deaths were in the hundreds.

sources.citation: ACLED Data for Sudan, 2020

2021.assessment: The military coup of October 25, 2021 triggered mass protests and security force crackdowns. Dozens of protesters killed in anti-coup demonstrations across November-December 2021. Inter-communal violence in Darfur (particularly West Darfur) continued to cause significant civilian casualties. ACLED recorded hundreds of fatalities across political and communal violence.

sources.citation: ACLED Data for Sudan, 2021

2022.assessment: Anti-coup protests continued throughout 2022 with security forces killing dozens of demonstrators. Inter-communal violence in Darfur (El Geneina, West Darfur) caused hundreds of civilian deaths with Masalit-Arab tribal clashes. ACLED recorded over 500 fatalities in organized violence. RSF involvement in communal violence in Darfur was documented.

sources.citation: ACLED Data for Sudan, 2022

2023.assessment: Full-scale SAF-RSF war erupted April 15, 2023 in Khartoum and spread rapidly across the country. The fighting in Darfur caused mass atrocities, particularly the RSF massacre in El Geneina (West Darfur) killing thousands of Masalit civilians. ACLED and UN estimates suggest tens of thousands of conflict deaths in 2023 across Sudan, making it one of the world's worst humanitarian crises.

sources.citation: ACLED Data for Sudan, 2023
UN OCHA Sudan Crisis Update, 2023

2024.assessment: SAF-RSF war continued throughout 2024. Darfur experienced ongoing mass atrocities with RSF attacks on civilian populations. El Fasher (North Darfur) remained besieged. The UN estimated over 150,000 conflict deaths by 2024, with Sudan having the world's largest humanitarian crisis. Famine conditions emerged in conflict areas.

sources.citation: ACLED Data for Sudan, 2024; UN OCHA Sudan Crisis Reports, 2024

2025.assessment: The SAF-RSF war continued into 2025. SAF launched counteroffensives including in the Khartoum area. Fighting continued in Darfur and Kordofan. No ceasefire agreement was reached. The humanitarian crisis deepened with widespread famine. Conflict deaths continued at high rates though the pace of fighting fluctuated.

sources.citation: Reuters News, Sudan war updates 2025

2004.assessment: Sudan under Bashir's NCP regime faced the Darfur conflict starting 2003, with large portions of western Sudan outside effective government control. The SPLA-N maintained areas in the south. Central government controlled Khartoum and major urban centers but fighting in Darfur made vast areas contested.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Sudan: Towards an Incomplete Peace, December 2003

2005.assessment: The Comprehensive Peace Agreement (CPA) was signed in January 2005, ending the north-south civil war and establishing a framework for southern autonomy. However, the Darfur conflict continued with government and Janjaweed forces contesting rebel-held areas. Government nominal authority covered the north; the south fell under SPLM/A authority within the CPA framework.

sources.citation: UN Security Council Report on Sudan, 2005

2006.assessment: Darfur conflict continued with ongoing fighting between government forces, Janjaweed militias, and rebel movements. The Darfur Peace Agreement (DPA) was signed in May 2006 but only one rebel faction signed, leaving large areas contested. Southern Sudan operated under CPA interim autonomy with SPLM governance structures.

sources.citation: ICG Report: To Save Darfur, March 2006

2007.assessment: Darfur remained deeply contested with rebel factions controlling significant territory in western Sudan. UN-AU hybrid mission (UNAMID) authorized in July 2007. East Sudan witnessed the Eastern Sudan Peace Agreement holding but fragile. Southern Sudan under CPA framework with SPLM government.

sources.citation: UN Security Council Resolution 1769 authorizing UNAMID, July 2007

2008.assessment: Darfur conflict continued; major rebel attack on Omdurman (suburb of Khartoum) in May 2008 demonstrated government vulnerability even near the capital. UNAMID deployed but operations constrained by government. Southern Sudan CPA framework in place with relatively stable autonomy.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, Darfur 2008 Report

2009.assessment: Darfur conflict continued though at somewhat lower intensity; ICC issued arrest warrant for Bashir in March 2009 for war crimes. Southern Sudan preparing for 2011 independence referendum. Government expelled many international NGOs from Darfur following ICC warrant, further reducing humanitarian access and worsening governance gaps.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Sudan: Preventing Implosion, December 2009

2010.assessment: Sudan held elections in April 2010 (NCP won in flawed process). Darfur conflict continued with reduced intensity but substantial rebel-held territory. South Sudan CPA referendum scheduled for January 2011. East Sudan relatively stable. Government controlled major cities and north but Darfur remained contested.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2011, Sudan

2011.assessment: South Sudan voted overwhelmingly for independence in January 2011 referendum and became independent on July 9, 2011, reducing Sudan's territory by roughly one-third. The remaining Sudan government retained control of Khartoum and the north. New conflict erupted in South Kordofan (June 2011) and Blue Nile (September 2011) states as SPLM-N fought government forces, adding new contested areas.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Sudan: Regional Perspectives on the Prospect of Southern Independence, May 2011

2012.assessment: Post-South Sudan independence, Sudan faced active conflicts in South Kordofan, Blue Nile states (SPLM-N), and continued Darfur instability. Sudan-South Sudan border clashes in April 2012. Government controlled Khartoum and major cities in northern Sudan, but peripheral areas (Darfur, Nuba Mountains, Blue Nile) remained contested.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Encyclopedia, Sudan, 2012

2013.assessment: Multiple armed conflicts continued: Darfur (reduced from peak but ongoing), South Kordofan, Blue Nile. Government controlled major northern cities and core territory but large peripheral areas remained under rebel or mixed control. Abyei region remained disputed with South Sudan.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Sudan's Spreading Conflict, Part I: War in South Kordofan, February 2013

2014.assessment: Sudan continued to face insurgencies in Darfur, South Kordofan, and Blue Nile. Government forces used aerial bombardment in conflict areas. ICG estimated government controlled the north and major cities but significant portions of western and southern periphery remained contested or ungoverned.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Sudan's Spreading Conflict, Part III: The Limits of Darfur's Peace Process, January 2014

2015.assessment: Sudan's Bashir regime maintained control of Khartoum and the north while armed conflicts continued in Darfur, South Kordofan, and Blue Nile. Government forces launched offensives in Nuba Mountains. Peace talks in Addis Ababa failed to produce results. UNAMID continued deployment in Darfur.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, World Report 2016: Sudan

2016.assessment: Sudan government maintained control of major cities. Armed conflicts continued in Darfur, South Kordofan, and Blue Nile, though at reduced intensity compared to peak years. Government declared unilateral ceasefire in some conflict zones. Peripheral regions remained significantly outside effective government control.

sources.citation: ACLED Data for Sudan, 2016

2017.assessment: Sudan under Bashir government controlled core northern territory and all major cities. Armed conflicts in Darfur, South Kordofan, and Blue Nile continued at reduced levels. US partially lifted sanctions in October 2017, reflecting improved access. Peripheral areas including parts of Darfur, Nuba Mountains, and Blue Nile hills remained outside effective central authority.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Sudan and South Sudan's Merging Conflicts, January 2015 (updated context)

2018.assessment: By 2018, the government had military control of most conflict zones in Darfur, though ungoverned spaces and areas outside effective administration persisted. Popular protests began in December 2018 in cities. Government controlled all major urban centers and most of the north. Nuba Mountains and Blue Nile areas retained rebel presence.

sources.citation: ACLED Data for Sudan, 2018

2019.assessment: Bashir was ousted in April 2019 by the military after months of mass protests. The Transitional Sovereignty Council (TSC) assumed power, a hybrid civilian-military arrangement. State control across Sudan remained similar to the late Bashir period — government forces controlled major cities and northern Sudan, while Darfur and peripheral areas remained incompletely governed. The Juba Peace Agreement negotiations were underway with armed movements.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Sudan's Transition at Risk, December 2019

2020.assessment: The Juba Peace Agreement (JPA) was signed in October 2020 between the transitional government and major armed movements from Darfur, South Kordofan, and Blue Nile. This brought most armed groups nominally under a peace framework. However, implementation remained incomplete and armed groups retained de facto territorial presence in their home regions. Core government control remained in Khartoum and northern Sudan.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Safeguarding Sudan's Transition, October 2019 (updated with JPA context)

2021.assessment: General al-Burhan led a military coup in October 2021, dissolving the civilian-led transitional government. The SAF (Sudanese Armed Forces) and RSF (Rapid Support Forces) jointly controlled Khartoum and major northern cities. Peripheral areas (Darfur, South Kordofan, Blue Nile) remained partially outside complete central control despite JPA framework. RSF had significant autonomous presence in Darfur under Hemeti (Mohamed Hamdan Dagalo).

sources.citation: ICG Report: Sudan's Coup and the Perils Ahead, November 2021

2022.assessment: Military junta (al-Burhan's SAF and Hemeti's RSF in uneasy alliance) controlled Khartoum and major cities. Framework Agreement negotiations with civilian forces proceeded throughout 2022. Darfur and peripheral states had significant RSF presence that operated with considerable autonomy from formal SAF command. Civilian protests against military rule continued but control remained with military.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Sudan: Reversing the Dangerous Fragmentation of Power, December 2022

2023.assessment: War between SAF and RSF erupted in April 2023. By year-end, the RSF controlled most of Khartoum and large parts of Darfur (including all major towns in Darfur except El Fasher by late 2023), while SAF retained Omdurman areas and the north. The country was effectively split between two competing military authorities with no functioning central government.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Sudan's Spreading War, November 2023

2024.assessment: The SAF-RSF war continued throughout 2024. RSF expanded control across Darfur and held most of Khartoum. SAF retained Port Sudan (declared new de facto capital), most of the north, and Omdurman/Khartoum North areas. El Fasher in North Darfur was besieged by RSF. The country remained split with no central government controlling more than roughly half the territory.

sources.citation: ACLED Data for Sudan, 2024; Reuters reporting on Sudan war 2024

2025.assessment: The SAF-RSF civil war continued into 2025. SAF made some counteroffensives, including retaking parts of Khartoum in early 2025, but RSF remained entrenched in Darfur and parts of greater Khartoum. No ceasefire was in effect. The country remained effectively partitioned between two armed forces with no unified central government.

sources.citation: Reuters News, Sudan civil war reporting, 2025
