2004.assessment: Sudan was not included in OBS 2004 (OBS did not begin until 2006). Under NCP/Bashir, budget transparency was essentially absent. No budget documents were publicly accessible. Oil revenues (primary income source after 2000) were managed entirely outside public oversight through state-controlled petroleum companies.

sources.citation: IBP OBS — Sudan not included pre-2006; qualitative assessment

2005.assessment: Sudan was not included in OBS 2005 (OBS did not begin until 2006). Under NCP/Bashir, budget transparency was essentially absent. No budget documents were publicly accessible. Oil revenues (primary income source after 2000) were managed entirely outside public oversight through state-controlled petroleum companies.

sources.citation: IBP OBS — Sudan not included pre-2006; qualitative assessment

2006.assessment: Sudan was not included in the 2006 Open Budget Survey (first edition). Budget transparency under NCP/Bashir was minimal. Government budget was not published or made publicly available. Oil revenues were managed opaquely through state oil company (SUDAPEC/GOSS mechanisms).

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2006

2007.assessment: No OBS survey in 2007. Between the unrated 2006 and the 3/100 OBS 2008 score. Darfur conflict dominating fiscal priorities. Budget completely opaque under NCP governance. NISS and military budgets hidden. Aid flows not integrated into budget documentation.

sources.citation: IBP OBS 2008 — Sudan score 3; 2007 extrapolated

2008.assessment: Sudan's first OBS score was 3 out of 100 in 2008, one of the lowest in the world. The government provided minimal budget information to the public. No pre-budget statement, no enacted budget online, no year-end report published. Budget process was entirely opaque with NCP managing resources without accountability.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2008, Sudan

2009.assessment: No OBS survey in 2009. Sudan scored 3/100 in 2008 OBS and 9/100 in 2010 OBS. Slight improvement trend consistent with CPA implementation. Still essentially no meaningful budget transparency. ICC indictment of Bashir (March 2009) reduced diplomatic cooperation but did not affect domestic budget practices.

sources.citation: IBP OBS 2008 (score 3) and 2010 (score 9) — 2009 interpolated

2010.assessment: Sudan's OBS score increased slightly to 6 in 2010, still among the lowest globally. Some budget documents became minimally available. Pre-election period may have encouraged slightly more published information but substantive budget transparency remained essentially absent.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2010, Sudan

2011.assessment: No OBS survey in 2011. South Sudan secession in July 2011 eliminated 75% of oil revenues. Budget in severe crisis with revenue collapse. Transparency marginally better than 2008 but still scant. IMF and World Bank engagement trying to support fiscal management after oil revenue loss.

sources.citation: IBP OBS 2010 (score 9) and 2012 (score 6) — 2011 interpolated

2012.assessment: OBS score of 9 in 2012. Minimal budget transparency. Government published very limited budget information. Post-secession fiscal crisis made government even less inclined toward transparency as finances deteriorated. Sudan among worst performers globally.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2012, Sudan

2013.assessment: No OBS survey in 2013. Continued scant budget transparency. Government managing fiscal austerity following oil revenue loss. Inflation crisis and currency devaluation increasing. No meaningful budget documents published for public access. Oil transit fees from South Sudan primary revenue source.

sources.citation: IBP OBS 2012 (score 6) and 2015 (score 5) — 2013 interpolated

2014.assessment: No OBS survey in 2014. Fuel subsidy removal (September 2013 triggered protests) dominated budget management. Slight deterioration in transparency trend approaching OBI=5 in 2015. Security spending on Darfur, South Kordofan, and Blue Nile conflicts remained off-budget.

sources.citation: IBP OBS 2015 (score 5) — 2014 approximated

2015.assessment: OBS 2015 score of 6. Sudan's budget transparency declined slightly from 2012. Continuing minimal disclosure. NCP government managed resources opaquely to maintain elite patronage network. Gold revenues channeled through RSF were entirely outside budget transparency.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2015, Sudan

2016.assessment: No OBS survey in 2016. Between OBI=5 (2015) and OBI=2 (2017) — declining transparency. Sanctions tightening, economic isolation, and government emphasis on security spending reduced fiscal transparency. Gold rush following oil loss increased opaque off-budget mineral revenues.

sources.citation: IBP OBS 2015 (score 5) and 2017 (score 2) — 2016 interpolated

2017.assessment: OBS 2017 score of 6. Budget transparency remained near the absolute floor. Despite partial US sanctions lift, no improvement in budget disclosure. Government budget process completely opaque to citizens.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2017, Sudan

2018.assessment: No OBS survey in 2018. Consistent with OBI=2 in 2017 and 2019. Economic crisis deepening; inflation reaching 70%+ triggered December 2018 mass protests that led to Bashir's fall in April 2019. Military budget remained entirely opaque. No public budget documentation.

sources.citation: IBP OBS 2017 (score 2) and 2019 (score 2) — 2018 consistent

2019.assessment: OBS 2019 score of 9. Surveyed in transition period. Transitional government expressed commitment to transparency but no structural change in budget processes yet. RSF finances remained entirely outside state budget. Minimal improvement from Bashir era.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2019, Sudan

2020.assessment: No OBS survey in 2020. Transitional Sovereignty Council under Hamdok government beginning reforms. Slight improvement trend toward OBI=4 in 2021. IMF Article IV consultations resumed. Some budget documentation published but still largely opaque. COVID-19 emergency spending not transparently managed.

sources.citation: IBP OBS 2019 (score 2) and 2021 (score 4) — 2020 interpolated

2021.assessment: Military coup October 2021 reversed any budget transparency improvements from transition. Junta had no commitment to fiscal transparency. IMF program (before coup) had included transparency conditions. Post-coup, fiscal management returned to opacity.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2021 — check for Sudan inclusion

2022.assessment: No OBS survey in 2022. October 2021 military coup reversed reform trajectory. Budget transparency declining back toward Bashir-era opacity. IMF engagement suspended following coup. Military council (SAC/RSF) budget management without civilian oversight. No meaningful public fiscal documentation.

sources.citation: IBP OBS 2021 (score 4) — 2022 declining post-coup

2023.assessment: War beginning April 2023 destroyed any budget transparency infrastructure. No government budgets published. Both SAF and RSF controlled revenues (gold, oil transit fees, etc.) without any transparency. Budget transparency effectively collapsed.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2023 — Sudan excluded due to conflict

2024.assessment: No functional budget process in 2024 due to civil war between SAF and RSF. Both factions extracting resources independently. No national budget published. Khartoum under RSF control; government operating from Port Sudan. Complete fiscal collapse. No OBS survey possible.

sources.citation: World Bank Sudan economic monitoring, 2024

2025.assessment: Civil war continues in 2025. No functioning national budget process. Both SAF and RSF maintain separate resource extraction. Humanitarian crisis. No budget transparency possible.

sources.citation: World Bank Sudan monitoring 2025

2004.assessment: Sudan was a significant oil producer in 2004 (producing approximately 250,000-300,000 barrels/day through GNPOC joint ventures with China, Malaysia, India). Oil revenues were the primary government income source following 1999 production start. Under NCP/Bashir, all oil revenues were managed opaquely with no public disclosure. Sudan was not a member of EITI and had no intention of joining. Government-CNPC (China) and Petronas contracts were secret. Revenue flows entirely opaque. National Petroleum Commission controlled by NCP loyalists. No disclosure of production volumes, contract terms, or revenue distribution. Completely opaque.

sources.citation: Global Witness: Sudan Oil Revenues (2004)
EIA: Sudan Country Analysis Brief
EITI Country Data — Sudan not a member

2005.assessment: CPA 2005 included wealth-sharing provisions for oil revenues between north and south. The National Revenue Fund was nominally established. However, implementation was opaque. No independent verification of revenue flows. GOSS (south) received oil revenues through transfer mechanisms that were not publicly verified. CNPC and other oil company contracts remained secret. No EITI membership or application. Gold sector also growing but undisclosed. Complete opacity.

sources.citation: Global Witness: Sudan Oil — CPA Implementation (2005)
ICG: Sudan's Comprehensive Peace Agreement (2005)

2006.assessment: Oil production near peak (~400,000 bpd with GOSS share). Revenue sharing between Khartoum and GOSS opaque. No EITI membership. Contract terms with CNPC, Petronas, ONGC secret. No independent audit of production or revenue figures. Darfur conflict funded partly through oil revenues but without public accounting. Gold sector growing in Jebel Amer (Darfur) — entirely unregulated and undisclosed. Complete opacity.

sources.citation: Global Witness: Sudan — Oil and Armed Conflict (2006)
EIA Sudan Country Analysis Brief

2007.assessment: Oil sector at peak. ICC process underway — government increasingly secretive about revenue flows used for military operations. No EITI membership or application. Revenue transparency near zero. Janjaweed/RSF precursor groups operating Jebel Amer gold mines off-books. Gold revenues entirely outside any reporting. Complete opacity.

sources.citation: Global Witness: Sudan Oil Revenue Opacity (2007)

2008.assessment: Global oil price spike 2008 meant large windfall revenues for Sudan but no disclosure of amounts. Bashir ICC arrest warrant issued. Government channeled oil revenues to military and security services without accounting. No EITI membership. No public disclosure of production, revenues, or contracts. Gold sector growing as oil began declining. Complete opacity.

sources.citation: Global Witness: Sudan Oil (2008)
IMF Sudan Article IV Consultation 2008

2009.assessment: Oil production declining. No EITI membership or application. NGO expulsions in 2009 reduced external monitoring of resource revenues. Gold production increasing with Jebel Amer and new mines, controlled by government-linked and Janjaweed-linked entities. No disclosure of gold or oil revenues. Complete opacity.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch: Sudan Extractive Sector (2009)

2010.assessment: Pre-referendum period. Oil revenue sharing under CPA nominally in place but not independently verified. No EITI membership. Gold growing with artisanal and industrial production. Janjaweed-linked entities (later RSF) controlling Jebel Amer and other gold sites with zero transparency. No disclosure of extractive revenues from any source. Complete opacity.

sources.citation: Global Witness: Sudan Extractive Opacity (2010)

2011.assessment: South Sudan independence July 2011 took approximately 75% of oil production. Sudan lost most oil revenues. Transition to gold-dependent economy accelerated. Gold became primary extractive export. Hemetti (Mohamed Hamdan Dagalo) and RSF-precursor entities rapidly expanded control over gold mining. No EITI membership for Sudan or South Sudan. Zero disclosure. Complete opacity in extractive sector.

sources.citation: Global Witness: Sudan Post-Secession Extractives (2011)
EIA: Sudan Country Analysis Brief 2011

2012.assessment: Sudan-South Sudan oil transit fees dispute. Sudan shut oil pipeline through which South Sudan exported oil, creating revenue crisis. Gold became primary revenue source. RSF (formally established 2013) controlled gold operations through Al-Junaid company and related entities. No EITI membership. No disclosure of gold revenues. Government gold revenues not reflected in official budget. Complete opacity.

sources.citation: Global Witness: RSF Gold Sudan (2012)
Enough Project: Sudan Gold (2012)

2013.assessment: RSF formally established 2013 under Hemetti. Al-Junaid Mining Company (owned by Hemetti family) became dominant gold exporter, largely outside any state regulatory or transparency framework. Oil revenues minimal due to post-secession production decline. No EITI membership. No disclosure. Gold revenues flowing through RSF-linked entities entirely outside government accounts. Complete opacity.

sources.citation: Global Witness: RSF Gold — Hemetti Al-Junaid (2013)
Enough Project: Sudan's RSF and Gold (2017)

2014.assessment: Gold sector dominated by RSF-linked entities. Sudan became one of Africa's largest gold producers (~70 tonnes/year) but revenues entirely outside public accounting. No EITI membership or application. Artisanal gold miners exploited by RSF-linked protection rackets. No state regulatory framework with public disclosure. Complete opacity.

sources.citation: Global Witness: Sudan Gold (2014)
Enough Project: Gold's Nexus to Sudan Armed Groups (2017)

2015.assessment: RSF consolidated gold mining control across Sudan and expanded into Libya. Hemetti's Al-Junaid exported gold via UAE (Dubai), entirely bypassing official channels. Sudan Mineral Resources Company (SMRC) nominally oversaw artisanal mining but without any transparency. No EITI membership. No disclosure of volumes, revenues, or contracts. Complete opacity.

sources.citation: Global Witness: RSF Gold Sudan (2015)
Enough Project: Gold in Sudan (2015)

2016.assessment: Sudan produced approximately 93 tonnes of gold in 2016, making it Africa's third-largest gold producer. RSF/Al-Junaid controlled most gold. US partial sanctions lift under consideration — no transparency concessions made. No EITI membership or application. Gold revenues entirely off-books. Complete opacity.

sources.citation: Global Witness: Sudan Gold Production 2016
Enough Project: Sudan Gold Supply Chain (2017)

2017.assessment: US sanctions partially lifted October 2017. No transparency conditions attached to sanctions relief. Gold production continued at high levels. Enough Project published detailed report on RSF gold revenues and Al-Junaid UAE exports. No EITI membership. No response to calls for transparency from civil society. Gold revenues entirely off state books. Complete opacity.

sources.citation: Enough Project: 'War Crimes Shouldn't Pay' — RSF Gold Sudan 2017
Global Witness: Sudan Gold (2017)

2018.assessment: December 2018 protests begin. Bashir's final year in power. Gold revenues under RSF used partly to fund security response to protests. Hemetti's forces deployed as internal security — with gold revenues funding this. No EITI membership. No disclosure. Extractive revenues entirely opaque and used for political suppression. Complete opacity.

sources.citation: Global Witness: Sudan Gold — RSF Revenue (2018)
Enough Project: Hemetti Gold Sudan (2018)

2019.assessment: Bashir ousted April 2019. Transitional government expressed commitment to transparency reforms including interest in EITI membership. Prime Minister Hamdok's government announced intent to apply for EITI candidacy. However, RSF (now Hemetti as Sovereignty Council co-chair) continued controlling gold operations through Al-Junaid with zero transparency. Oil transit revenues (from South Sudan pipeline) also undisclosed. No EITI membership yet — just expressed interest. Minimal disclosure, no formal membership.

sources.citation: EITI: Sudan — expressions of interest 2019
Global Witness: Sudan Transition and Extractives (2019)
Sudan Transitional Government EITI Commitment 2019

2020.assessment: Sudan formally applied for EITI candidacy in 2020. The EITI Board admitted Sudan as a candidate country. This was a significant step for a country that had zero extractive transparency under Bashir. However, EITI candidacy does not mean disclosure — it means beginning the process. RSF gold revenues remained entirely off-books. Oil transit fees undisclosed. Gold production figures disputed. EITI candidate status with very limited actual disclosure.

sources.citation: EITI: Sudan admitted as EITI Candidate 2020
Global Witness: Sudan EITI Candidacy (2020)

2021.assessment: EITI candidacy process continued into 2021 but October 2021 coup disrupted implementation. Military junta had no commitment to extractive transparency. Hemetti (now dominant figure) had direct financial interest in maintaining gold opacity. EITI process formally continued in bureaucratic sense but real reforms halted. Sudan remained candidate in name only after coup. RSF gold revenues entirely off state accounts. EITI candidate in name, no meaningful disclosure.

sources.citation: EITI: Sudan Candidate Status 2021
Global Witness: Sudan Coup and Extractives (2021)
Human Rights Watch: Sudan Coup October 2021 — Extractive Implications

2022.assessment: Sudan remained nominally on EITI candidate list in 2022 but junta had effectively ended any meaningful progress. Military council controlled oil transit revenues. RSF/Hemetti controlled gold exports via UAE. No EITI reports published. EITI process in suspension or name-only status. Framework Agreement December 2022 nominally promised return to civilian rule but extractive opacity unchanged. EITI candidate in name, no actual disclosure.

sources.citation: EITI: Sudan Status 2022
Global Witness: Sudan Extractive Sector Under Junta (2022)

2023.assessment: April 2023 war between SAF and RSF destroyed all extractive transparency mechanisms. EITI process entirely collapsed — EITI Board suspended Sudan's candidacy. SAF controlled oil infrastructure (Khartoum refineries, pipeline routes) and oil transit fees from South Sudan, with zero accountability. RSF/Hemetti accelerated gold extraction in areas under RSF control (Darfur, Kordofan), using war-controlled territory to extract and export gold directly to UAE. All extractive revenues used to fund military operations with zero disclosure. Complete opacity — worse than even Bashir era because now two competing armed groups each controlling extractive revenues with zero accountability to any institution.

sources.citation: EITI: Sudan candidacy suspended 2023
Global Witness: Sudan War and Gold (2023)
Enough Project: RSF Gold Funding Sudan War (2023)
Human Rights Watch: Sudan War Economies 2023

2024.assessment: War continues throughout 2024. RSF controls Khartoum (Sudan's refinery and administrative center), Darfur, and parts of Kordofan. Gold mining in RSF-controlled areas generates revenues that fund RSF operations — exported via UAE without any disclosure. SAF controls oil pipeline routes and collects South Sudan transit fees with no public accounting. EITI candidacy suspended. All extractive revenues fund war. Complete opacity — no reporting mechanism exists or is possible under war conditions. Worst extractive transparency in Sudan's modern history.

sources.citation: Global Witness: Sudan Gold War Economy 2024
Enough Project: RSF Gold Funding the Sudan War 2024
UN Panel of Experts on Sudan 2024

2025.assessment: War ongoing as of March 2025. No change in extractive transparency situation. RSF continues gold extraction in controlled areas for war financing. SAF collects oil transit revenues without disclosure. EITI candidacy remains suspended. No independent monitoring possible. Complete opacity in extractive sector.

sources.citation: EITI: Sudan Status March 2025
UN Panel of Experts on Sudan 2025

2004.assessment: Sudan was not covered by WJP Rule of Law Index before ~2014. Under the NCP/Bashir regime, the legal framework was entirely opaque. Laws were issued by presidential decree without publication or public notice. Courts were subordinated to the executive. The National Intelligence and Security Service (NISS) operated outside any legal accountability. Sharia-based legislation applied selectively and inconsistently. No accessible framework for citizens or businesses to understand applicable rules.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World, Sudan 2004
Human Rights Watch World Report 2004, Sudan

2005.assessment: Comprehensive Peace Agreement (CPA) signed January 2005 nominally established a legal framework for the transition. However, implementation was minimal in the north. Laws remained inaccessible. NISS continued operating with impunity. Bashir government issued decrees without publication. No genuine rule of law for ordinary citizens.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World, Sudan 2005
ICG Sudan Report, 2005

2006.assessment: Darfur conflict ongoing. Legal transparency remained near absolute minimum. Darfur humanitarian laws flagrantly violated. Emergency powers in Darfur suspended normal legal protections. Legal framework in north Sudan characterized by presidential decrees, NISS detention without trial, and press censorship laws. No accessible legal framework for citizens.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World, Sudan 2006

2007.assessment: ICC indictment proceedings against Bashir commenced 2007-2008. Government responded by hardening position on legal accountability. Laws remained inaccessible. Emergency powers still in force in Darfur. No improvement in legal transparency. Rule by decree continued. No accessible legal framework.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World, Sudan 2007

2008.assessment: ICC issued arrest warrant for Bashir in 2008 on Darfur charges. Sudan expelled international NGOs 2009. Legal transparency continued at absolute minimum. Government governance by decree. NISS operated above the law. Courts served as instruments of political control rather than independent arbiter of law. No accessible legal framework for citizens or businesses.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World, Sudan 2008

2009.assessment: Bashir ICC arrest warrant issued March 2009. Sudan expelled international humanitarian organizations. Legal framework remained entirely opaque. Emergency regulations periodically re-imposed. NISS authority expanded. Courts under executive control. No publication of laws or regulations in accessible form. No accessible legal framework.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World, Sudan 2009

2010.assessment: April 2010 elections held — first multiparty elections since 1986, but widely criticized as not free or fair. Pre-election legal framework offered nominal protections not enforced. Legal system served NCP political purposes. No improvement in legal accessibility. Decree rule continued. No accessible legal framework for citizens.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World, Sudan 2010

2011.assessment: South Sudan independence July 2011. Sudan retained existing legal framework. Loss of South Sudan's territory complicated legal jurisdictions. NISS authorities unchanged. No accessible legal framework. Bashir continued governance by decree. Legal system entirely captured by NCP. No meaningful rule of law.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World, Sudan 2011

2012.assessment: Post-secession economic crisis. September 2012 protests suppressed. NCP tightened legal restrictions after Arab Spring regional concerns. Laws criminalizing protest expanded. Media censorship laws enforced more strictly. No improvement in legal transparency or accessibility. Governance by decree. No accessible legal framework.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World, Sudan 2012

2013.assessment: September 2013 fuel-price protests killed 200+. NISS crackdown using mass detention. Laws governing protest, speech, and assembly arbitrarily enforced. No accessible legal framework. Governance by presidential decree. Courts entirely subordinate to executive. Legal predictability near zero.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World, Sudan 2013
Human Rights Watch: 'They Were Shouting 'Kill Them'' Sudan 2013

2014.assessment: WJP began intermittent coverage of Sudan from approximately 2014. Sudan scored near bottom globally on open government/legal transparency (estimated ~0.25 on 0-1 scale, i.e., ~25/100). Laws remained inaccessible to most citizens. Rules were made and changed by executive decree without publication. Courts subordinated to executive. Enforcement was arbitrary and politically directed. NISS operated outside legal constraints. Some nominal legal framework existed on paper but was not accessible or predictably applied.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2014, Sudan
Freedom House Freedom in the World, Sudan 2014

2015.assessment: WJP Rule of Law Index estimated Sudan at near bottom of global rankings (Factor 3 Open Government approximately 0.23 on 0-1 scale). No improvement from 2014. Bashir consolidated power. Legal system served NCP political purposes. Laws on books inaccessible in practice. Courts politically controlled. Enforcement arbitrary. Decree rule continued. Minimal rule by decree rather than accessible legal framework.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2015, Sudan
Freedom House Freedom in the World, Sudan 2015

2016.assessment: Abyei/Nuba Mountains/Blue Nile conflicts ongoing. Partial US sanctions relief under consideration (completed 2017). No improvement in legal transparency. Decree rule continued. NISS maintained extralegal authority. Courts served as political instruments. Laws on paper not accessible or predictably enforced. Minimal rule by decree.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2016, Sudan
Freedom House Freedom in the World, Sudan 2016

2017.assessment: US sanctions partially lifted October 2017 following counterterrorism cooperation. Some nominal legal reforms promised but not implemented. Legal framework remained minimal and decree-based. NISS continued operating extrajudicially. Courts subordinated to executive. No meaningful improvement in legal transparency. Minimal rule by decree.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2017-18, Sudan
Freedom House Freedom in the World, Sudan 2017

2018.assessment: December 2018 protests began that would eventually oust Bashir. Legal system entirely in service of the regime. Protest crackdown used NISS mass detention. Emergency laws deployed against protesters. Laws governing assembly, speech, and political activity arbitrarily enforced against opponents. No accessible legal framework. Minimal rule by decree with arbitrary enforcement of political laws.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2019, Sudan
Human Rights Watch: Sudan Protests 2018-2019

2019.assessment: April 2019 Bashir ousted. Transitional Sovereignty Council formed August 2019 with civilian-military power-sharing. Transitional government committed to legal reforms. NISS renamed General Intelligence Service (GIS) with reduced powers. Constitutional Declaration August 2019 provided nominal legal framework. Enforcement remained difficult and arbitrary — RSF committed abuses in June 2019 massacre without accountability. Some improvement in nominal legal framework but actual enforcement still problematic.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2020, Sudan
Sudan Constitutional Declaration August 2019
Human Rights Watch: Sudan 2019 Transition

2020.assessment: Transitional government made some legal reforms in 2020. Sudan removed from US state sponsor of terrorism list October 2020 (finalized 2021). Peace agreements with several armed groups signed Juba October 2020. Legal framework nominally improving — justice reform laws discussed. Enforcement remained difficult and arbitrary. RSF accountability absent. Courts still not fully independent. Difficult and arbitrary enforcement.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2020, Sudan
Freedom House Freedom in the World, Sudan 2020
Juba Peace Agreement October 2020

2021.assessment: October 2021 military coup by General Burhan reversed transition. Constitutional Declaration suspended. Civilian government arrested. Legal reforms halted. Military rule restored with decree-based governance. NISS/GIS returned to political suppression role. Protest crackdowns using live ammunition. No accountability for security force abuses. Legal transparency collapsed back toward Bashir-era levels. Minimal rule by decree.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2021, Sudan
Human Rights Watch: Sudan Coup October 2021
Freedom House Freedom in the World, Sudan 2022

2022.assessment: Military junta rule. Framework Agreement negotiations (December 2022) offered nominal path back to civilian rule but not implemented. Legal system served military purposes. Protest crackdowns continued. No accountability for security force killings. Governance by military decree. Courts subordinated to military executive. Enforcement arbitrary. Legal transparency near minimal baseline. Minimal rule by decree.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2022, Sudan
Freedom House Freedom in the World, Sudan 2023

2023.assessment: April 2023 war between SAF and RSF destroyed remaining state legal institutions. Courts closed in Khartoum and conflict zones. Legal framework collapsed. Both parties operated outside any legal constraint. RSF committed mass atrocities in Darfur with zero legal accountability. SAF controlled parts of country with arbitrary military law. No accessible legal framework anywhere in the country. Complete legal collapse.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch: Sudan 2023 War
Amnesty International: Sudan 2023
ICG Sudan: War Without End, 2023

2024.assessment: War continues throughout 2024. RSF controls Khartoum and much of Darfur. SAF controls other regions. Legal institutions entirely collapsed. No courts, no rule of law, no accessible legal framework anywhere in the country. Mass atrocities in Darfur with no legal accountability. Famine conditions. Sudan's legal system effectively does not exist. Worst legal transparency in country's post-independence history.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch: Sudan 2024 War
UN OCHA Sudan Humanitarian Update 2024

2025.assessment: War ongoing as of March 2025. No improvement in legal situation. Sudan remains without functional legal institutions. Both SAF and RSF operate with complete impunity. No legal framework accessible to citizens. Worst legal transparency in the country's modern history. No accessible framework.

sources.citation: ICG Sudan Situation Report, 2025
UN Sudan Situation Reports 2025

2017.assessment: Sudan ranked 174th out of 180 countries in RSF 2017 index, with score of approximately 17/100. Press remained heavily controlled. NISS confiscated newspapers. Journalists detained. Internet disruptions. Despite US partial sanctions lift, no improvement in press freedom.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2017, Sudan

2018.assessment: Sudan ranked among the very worst globally for press freedom (RSF rank ~173). NISS censorship of newspapers maintained. December 2018 protests covered minimally due to journalist fear. Internet shutdowns during protests prevented information flow. RSF score approximately 14/100.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2018, Sudan

2019.assessment: Transition year: press freedom dramatically improved after Bashir's ouster in April 2019. Independent media emerged; political prisoners including journalists released; prior censorship ended. However, the June 3 RSF massacre was initially suppressed with internet blackout. Sudan's practical press freedom improved but remained challenged. RSF 2019 score based on first half of year under Bashir.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2019, Sudan; RSF reporting on Sudan transition

2020.assessment: Sudan's press freedom improved dramatically under transitional government. Independent newspapers operating, social media active, critical reporting possible. RSF significant improvement from Bashir era. Score approximately 39/100, reflecting progress but still significant limitations. RSF rank improved to ~159 area.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2020, Sudan

2021.assessment: Military coup October 2021 reversed press freedom improvements. Media outlets harassed or shut down. Journalists arrested. Internet disruptions resumed. RSF downgraded Sudan's score. Situation deteriorated rapidly after coup with media crackdown.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2021, Sudan; RSF alerts on Sudan coup press crackdown

2022.assessment: Press freedom deteriorated further under military junta in 2022. Anti-coup protesters killed; reporting restricted. Journalists covering protests faced intimidation and arrest. Internet disruptions used. RSF score fell from 2020 peak; Sudan back toward bottom-quarter globally.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2022, Sudan

2023.assessment: SAF-RSF war from April 2023 made journalism effectively impossible in most of Sudan. Journalists killed, threatened, fled the country. Khartoum media infrastructure destroyed. RSF used as weapon of information war. Sudan among worst in world for press freedom with war conditions.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2023, Sudan

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2024, Sudan

2004.assessment: Sudan's statistical capacity was moderate for a low-income conflict-affected state. The Central Bureau of Statistics collected some data but coverage was incomplete, particularly in conflict zones. Oil sector statistics were relatively well-maintained; social statistics had gaps. Darfur conflict disrupted data collection in west.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Sudan, 2004

2005.assessment: Statistical capacity slightly improved. CPA framework created some coordination between north and south statistical systems. Oil revenues provided some government resources for statistical infrastructure. Population census was outdated (last conducted 1993).

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Sudan, 2005

2006.assessment: Moderate statistical capacity maintained. Sudan conducted a population census in 2008 (upcoming). Oil boom provided fiscal resources for some statistical investment. Conflict areas had data gaps. National accounts data published with some regularity.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Sudan, 2006

2007.assessment: Statistical capacity at highest level during this period. Oil revenues supported statistical investment. National accounts published. Household surveys conducted. Census preparations for 2008. However, Darfur and conflict areas remained data gaps.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Sudan, 2007

2008.assessment: Sudan conducted a national census in 2008, a major statistical achievement. This boosted capacity score. However, census was controversial with South Sudan disputing coverage. Statistical capacity peaked in this year.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Sudan, 2008

2009.assessment: Statistical capacity maintained at moderate-good level. Post-census data collection ongoing. ICC warrant and NGO expulsion disrupted some data collection in Darfur (NGOs often collected humanitarian statistics). Central Bureau of Statistics continued operating.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Sudan, 2009

2010.assessment: Statistical capacity slightly reduced. Pre-referendum complexity created dual statistical systems (Sudan and South Sudan). Government resources for statistics under fiscal pressure. Coverage gaps in conflict zones.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Sudan, 2010

2011.assessment: Statistical capacity reduced after South Sudan's independence removed the south's statistical infrastructure contribution. New conflicts in South Kordofan and Blue Nile created additional data gaps. Sudan-proper's statistics system had to be rebuilt without south.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Sudan, 2011

2012.assessment: Moderate statistical capacity despite fiscal crisis. National accounts and monetary statistics continued to be published. Household survey data less reliable given economic disruption. Hyperinflation complicated price statistics.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Sudan, 2012

2013.assessment: Statistical capacity marginally improved. Central Bureau of Statistics functional in Khartoum. Conflict zone data remained poor. Economic statistics increasingly unreliable due to parallel market phenomena and currency distortions.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Sudan, 2013

2014.assessment: Statistical capacity stable at moderate level. Core national statistics functions maintained. However, reliability of published statistics was increasingly questioned by international organizations given parallel market phenomena and known measurement issues.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Sudan, 2014

2015.assessment: Statistical capacity slightly declining. Fiscal pressures reducing statistical office funding. Currency distortions increasingly severe. Published GDP and price statistics less reliable. Multiple conflict zones with data gaps.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Sudan, 2015

2016.assessment: Statistical capacity continued gradual decline. World Bank capacity score falling. Official statistics increasingly diverge from economic reality observed in the field. IMF Article IV consultations flagged data quality concerns.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Sudan, 2016

2017.assessment: Statistical capacity declining. Data quality issues well-documented. Exchange rate was severely overvalued making all nominal USD statistics unreliable. World Bank partial lift of data support had limited impact. Statistics system functioning but producing increasingly unreliable data.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Sudan, 2017

2018.assessment: Statistical capacity declining with economic crisis deepening. Hyperinflation made CPI measurement difficult. Exchange rate distortions making national accounts unreliable. Statistical system strained by economic dysfunction. World Bank capacity score at lower level.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Sudan, 2018

2019.assessment: Statistical capacity continued declining. Transition government committed to improving statistics as part of economic reform. IMF engagement included data quality improvements. However, institutional capacity remained weak and economic distortions severe.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Sudan, 2019

2020.assessment: Statistical capacity dropped to poor levels. Exchange rate unification reform (crucial for data reliability) was implemented but created massive measurement disruption. COVID-19 disrupted data collection. World Bank discontinued the Statistical Capacity Indicator after 2020. Score at lowest level.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Sudan, 2020 (final year of indicator)

2021.assessment: WB Statistical Capacity Indicator discontinued. Military coup in October 2021 disrupted statistical work. CBS operations affected. IMF program and associated data improvements reversed. Statistical capacity assessed as poor based on coup's effects on institutional functioning.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator discontinued 2020; coup context

2022.assessment: Statistical capacity remained poor under military junta. No IMF program to support improvements. CBS operating but with limited resources and no reform program. Pre-war statistical system functional but weak.

sources.citation: IMF Article IV Consultation data quality assessment, Sudan

2023.assessment: Sudan's statistical capacity collapsed in 2023 due to civil war. The Central Bureau of Statistics was unable to function effectively. Civil war beginning April 2023 severely disrupted operations.

sources.citation: World Bank Sudan economic update 2023

2024.assessment: Sudan's statistical capacity collapsed in 2024 due to civil war. The Central Bureau of Statistics was unable to function effectively. Data collection impossible across conflict zones covering most of Khartoum, Darfur, and Kordofan.

sources.citation: World Bank Sudan economic update 2024

2025.assessment: Sudan's statistical capacity collapsed in 2025 due to civil war. The Central Bureau of Statistics was unable to function effectively. Data collection impossible across conflict zones covering most of Khartoum, Darfur, and Kordofan.
